utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey jean how're you doing today

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm doing alright aside from this foot pain that i have

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so i see here that you looks like you hurt your left foot here where you were playing soccer can you tell me a little bit more about what happened

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah so yeah i was playing in a soccer game yesterday and i was trying to steal the ball from another player and she ended up falling directly onto my right foot and i do n't know i i mean i was trying to get around her and my body ended up twisting around her and then i accidentally felt a pain in my foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so have you ever hurt your left foot before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i've had a lot of injuries in soccer but never injured this foot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then so after the fall and the entanglement with the other player were you able to continue playing

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i had to stop playing right away and actually being helped off the field

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow okay and what have you been doing for the the pain since then

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i've been keeping it elevated icing it the trainer wrapped it yesterday and taking ibuprofen as well

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so without any ibuprofen can you tell me what your pain level is

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: without ibuprofen i would say my pain is a three

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then with your ibuprofen can you tell me what your pain level is

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: like a seven eight

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so how long have you been playing soccer

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: really since i was like four five i've been playing a long time

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well that's cool yeah we our our youngest daughter she is almost sixteen and she plays the inner marrial soccer league they are down at the rex center did is that where you started playing or did you guys did you start playing somewhere else

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah just like this local town leak i started playing that way and then played all throughout school

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: high school teams

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's awesome so just out of curiosity with the left foot have you experienced anything like numbness or tingling or or any strange sensation

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i have not

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay now if it's okay with you i would like to do a quick physical exam i reviewed your vitals and everything looks good blood pressure was one eighteen over seventy two heart rate was fifty eight respiratory rate was fourteen you are afebrile and you had an o2 saturation of ninety nine percent on room air on your heart exam your regular of rate and rhythm do n't appreciate any clicks rubs or murmurs no ectopic beats noted there on auscultation listening to your lungs lungs are clear and equal bilaterally so you're moving good air i'd like to do a focused foot exam on your left foot so i do see some bruising on the bottom of your foot and on the top of your foot as well now there is associated swelling and i do appreciate tenderness to palpation of your midfoot and you are positive for the piano key test on a neurovascular exam of your left foot you have a brisk capillary refill of less than three seconds dorsalis pedis pulse is intact and strong and you do have motor and sensation that it's intact to light touch now i would like to do a review of the diagnostic imaging that you had before you came in so i do notice a subtle dorsal displacement of the base of the second metatarsal with a three millimeter separation of the first and second metatarsal bases and the presence of a bony fragment in the lisfranc joint space so lem me talk to you a little bit about my assessment and plan now for for the first concern of right foot pain your right foot pain is due to a lisfranc fracture which is a fracture to one of your second metatarsal bones at the top of your foot where the metatarsals meet your cuboids now there are ligaments at the top of your foot so i'm gon na be ordering an mri to assess for injury to any of these ligaments now based on your exam and from what i'm seeing on your x-ray you're most likely going to need surgery of that foot now the surgery will place the bones back in their proper position and using plates and screws will hold them there while they heal and this is gon na allow those bones and ligaments to heal properly it is a day surgery and you will be able to go home the same day and then i'm going to have you follow up with me here in the clinic you'll be in a cast and you will need to use crutches and you will not be able to use that left foot for about six to eight weeks now after that six to eight weeks you will gradually start walking on your foot based on how you tolerate it and we'll see how you do at that point so i do believe you're gon na need surgery i i'm recommending this because there are significant complications to your foot if we do not do this poor bone and ligament healing can lead to losing the arch of your foot and you're becoming flat-footed you also have a high likelihood of developing arthritis in that foot so what i'm gon na do unfortunately you'll be out the rest of the season but we are gon na get you fixed up and ready for next season if you're okay with all of this i'm gon na have the nurse come in and get you started on your paperwork and then i will see you on monday morning and we will get your foot taken care of

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: alright thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're welcome

detail_list.summary: Left foot pain
Jean Martinez is a 27-year-old female presenting with left foot pain sustained during soccer.
The doctor asks if the patient has previously injured her left foot.
The patient reports a history of soccer injuries but denies prior left foot injury.
The doctor asks if the patient continued playing after the injury.
The patient stopped playing immediately and required assistance off the field.
The doctor asks about treatments the patient has used for her pain.
The patient has used elevation, ice, a wrap, and ibuprofen.
The doctor asks the patient to rate her pain without medication.
The patient rates her pain as 3/10 without medication.
The doctor asks the patient to rate her pain with medication.
The patient rates her pain as 7-8/10 with medication.
The doctor asks how long the patient has been playing soccer.
The patient has been playing soccer since age 4 or 5.
The patient plays in a local league and played throughout school.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced numbness or tingling in the left foot.
The patient denies numbness or tingling in the left foot.
The doctor reviews normal vitals (BP 118/72, HR 58, RR 14, afebrile, O2 99%) and normal heart and lung exams.
Physical exam of the left foot shows ecchymosis, swelling, midfoot tenderness, and a positive piano key test.
Neurovascular exam of the left foot shows brisk capillary refill, intact dorsalis pedis pulse, and intact motor/sensation.
Imaging shows dorsal displacement of the 2nd metatarsal base, 3mm separation of 1st and 2nd metatarsal bases, and a bony fragment in the Lisfranc joint.
Lisfranc fracture
The doctor will order an MRI to assess ligamentous injury.
The patient will likely require surgery to place bones in position with plates and screws.
The patient will be in a cast with crutches for 6-8 weeks, followed by gradual weight-bearing.
The patient is scheduled for surgery on Monday.
